medications.indication_rationale: First-line for acute urticaria; when a single standard dose fails, guideline step is to up-dose the non-sedating antihistamine (to 2–4× standard) rather than switch to a sedating agent. Source used cetirizine 20mg stat and fexofenadine 180mg up to BD.
medications.dose_route_frequency: e.g. cetirizine 10mg up to 40mg daily, or fexofenadine 180mg up to twice daily (as per source, off-label up-dosing)

medications.indication_rationale: A short course (e.g. prednisolone 25–50mg daily for 3 days) can help severe refractory acute flares per ASCIA; source used prednisolone 25mg daily for 3 days. Not required in all cases.
medications.dose_route_frequency: prednisolone 25mg daily for 3 days (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: sedating first-generation antihistamine (e.g. promethazine) as the escalation step
medications.indication_rationale: Switching to a sedating antihistamine instead of up-dosing the non-sedating one is not preferred — sedation, anticholinergic effects and impaired next-day function without better urticaria control.
Urticaria is not a bacterial infection; antibiotics are not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the antihistamine on a fixed regular schedule rather than waiting for the itch to peak.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Pre-emptive H1 blockade gives better control and reduces the scratch cycle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply cool compresses; avoid hot showers, tight clothing and vigorous exertion.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat and friction are physical aggravators of urticaria.

patient_education_points.point: This is hives (urticaria), often triggered by a recent viral infection. Individual welts fade within a day but new ones can keep appearing for days to a couple of weeks.

The key safety point: call 000 immediately and lie flat if you get any swelling of the lips/tongue/throat, a change in your voice, trouble breathing or swallowing, dizziness or faintness — these are signs of a severe allergic reaction.
Regular non-drowsy antihistamine is the mainstay; the dose can be increased if a standard dose isn't enough.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Rash resolution, itch control, any systemic features; consider GP review if persisting beyond 6 weeks (chronic urticaria).
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any lip/tongue/throat swelling, breathing difficulty, dizziness or faintness — call 000 immediately, do not wait for review.

must_include_items: Explicit anaphylaxis safety-net (lip/tongue/throat swelling, voice change, breathing difficulty, dizziness → 000, lie flat)
Regular non-sedating antihistamine, up-dosed if a standard dose fails
Reassurance that isolated acute urticaria is self-limiting and often post-viral
Cool compress / avoid heat & friction advice
acceptable_alternatives: Any 2nd-gen antihistamine (cetirizine, fexofenadine, loratadine, desloratadine) is acceptable
A short oral prednisolone course is acceptable for a severe flare but not mandatory
errors_of_omission: No advice to continue/up-dose antihistamine regularly
Failing to screen airway/CVS/GI before reassurance
errors_of_commission: Switching straight to a sedating antihistamine as the escalation step
Sending an isolated urticaria with a negative anaphylaxis screen to ED by ambulance
Prescribing adrenaline/EpiPen with no anaphylaxis features
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) an explicit anaphylaxis safety-net and (2) appropriate antihistamine management. Omitting the airway safety-net is a critical failure even though the baseline tier is low.
